text: The laparoscopic procedure involves the resection of Meckel's diverticulum to address small bowel volvulus, with the surgeon manipulating the bowel using laparoscopic instruments.

A thirty-five year old man presented with central abdominal pain and vomiting. A similar episode six months previously resolved spontaneously. On examination the abdomen was mildly distended and tender in the right iliac fossa. A CT scan showed a small bowel obstruction and was suggestive of a Meckel's diverticulum.
Following resuscitation, the patient was brought to the operating theatre for a laparoscopy.

The surgeon identifies a Meckel's diverticulum with a persistent vitelline ligament attached to the abdominal wall at the umbilicus, serving as the rotation point for a small bowel volvulus. The engorged segment of small bowel is reduced using atraumatic graspers, with no signs of ischemia visible.

The surgeon performs a thorough inspection of the small bowel from the duodenal jejunal ligament to the ileocecal valve, ensuring no additional pathology is present. With the volvulus reduced, the constricting effect on the small bowel mucin tree is visible.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to divide the vitelline ligament at the abdominal wall. The persistent vitelline artery supplying the Meckel's diverticulum is also ligated and divided with the harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic stapling device to resect the Meckel's diverticulum, ensuring the lumen of the ileum remains uncompromised. The resected specimen is placed into a laparoscopic retrieval bag and extracted through a ten millimeter port.
The patient made an uneventful post-operative recovery and was discharged home on the second post-operative day.
